txt: 'But I *am* interested,' you read from his face.
'Cut it out' is indeed what he is thinking.
*I will shield you from the elements and give my life for yours* -- that's what the cloak is relaying.

*IN MARTINAISE, LOOKING INTO KRENEL*, he writes in one. Then the man puts down his pen and rubs his temples with both hands. Outside there is a siren, distant gunshots on the streets of the Jamrock Quarter.
*Some* would still say you're a cop, but I guess we're beyond that now.
... but the optics on this are awful, he thinks. We need to be as silent as we can.
... is what you plan to say. Before you can get past 'you're under', the kid interrupts you:
... is what you plan to say. Before you can get past 'you're under', the lieutenant interrupts you:
... knows how to think this?
... lucky us.
...a long, long time ago.
...am I from? God, he doesn't *know*.
...and so all across Jamrock, Coal City, G.R.I.H. Forty two deceased persons found today -- forty two Stations of Breath.
...go ahead then, he thinks. As long as you can walk straight, I don't care.
...helping cryptozoologists isn't really a priority for our organization, is it? The lieutenant looks out the window, impatiently.
...if you must.
...in this case, we can't afford to be squeaky clean.
...let's see what's inside, he thinks.
...we shouldn't disrupt the way things are done here, he thinks. And have little power to do so anyway.
...what difference does it make?
...you won't get far without the lieutenant here to bounce thoughts off of -- where could he be right now? The asphalt passing underneath the front wheels of his motor carriage at a high speed...
A bond has been formed.
A camera -- an instant colour camera the lieutenant keeps tucked away in his coat pocket. A moan of discomfort sounds from the lazareth, as he turns onto his side...
A fortunate turn that the lieutenant isn't here. Imagine what he would have to say about this.
A joyrider jacket? You feel a strange connection to this joyrider. Maybe he's from some kind of Joyriders district and likes blue and white racing livery? Like a cop would.
A lowset house, windswept -- and without you. Traffic whooshes by...

A man and a woman sit in the front seat of an armoured motor carriage. The woman is driving, the man lights a cigarette. Jean Vicquemare is his name, the asphalt vanishes under the wheels of the machine. Ahead, harbour cranes rise to the sky...
A man of his word, this killer of killers.
A real-life procedural, complete with gory details...

A scrawny old man sits in a dusty cubicle, smoking, with a large white rectangle sewn on his vest. In front of him is a box-shaped apparatus with an array of wires and antennas -- the radio switchboard.
A white lie -- of course he has opinions.
ARRIVING
Across the bay, two figures watch the scene unfold through coin-operated binoculars. "My god," one says in a concerned voice. "Let me see," says the other.
Across the city, the lieutenant stops to consider a smoke break. He shrugs off the urge: maybe tonight. His absence is palpable. Too late now.

Across the water, on a dilapidated jetty in a nameless village -- made of grey cinder block houses with eternite roofs -- two police officers and one special consultant look across a narrow strip of sea.
All right. Let's see where this is going.
Am I mildly worried he might take it *seriously*, the lieutenant thinks. Yes. I am -- he concludes. I *am* worried.

An angry cop refusing to face his demons and taking his frustration out on others -- just a badge and a gun away from those he's manhandling. A fast burning candle -- that's the side you just exposed to the lieutenant. He doesn't judge, has seen it too many times all ready -- you both have probably, but he takes a mental note.
An extremely unprofessional and *hurtful* stereotype that's offensive to all upstanding officers of the law.
An instant camera -- the Trigat Sunshine Mini. A moan of discomfort sounds from the lazareth, as he turns onto his side...
An unfortunate choice of words, the lieutenant from Precinct 57 thinks. He looks at the harbour walls looming overhead. He could use a cigarette right about now.
An unfortunate choice of words, the lieutenant from Precinct 57 thinks. He looks downwind at the rotting corpse. He could use a cigarette right about now.
And I don't use the *mad* lightly, he thinks.
And Station 41 and the glow of its desk lamps, the clicking of coffee mugs, clouds of cigarette smoke and the ringing telephone... your youth. There is still a way back.
And Titus Hardie himself would make a good officer. That man was born to lead.
And he still isn't. People aren't afraid of good cops in the way Ruby was afraid of you, he thinks, then turns back to his men.
And he will help.
And mine too, he thinks -- but keep on. This must be done.
And the lieutenant too. And the men behind your back, drawing their weapons... you can live. You can get out of this.
And where is your posse, detective? Nothing comes to you. The world is silent.
And yet you can't help but sense a slight disappointment on his part. It seems a part of him hoped you would hit the pretentious young communist...
Another life...
Apart from the Union themselves of course.
Are there more terrible words to hear from your half-brother?
As long as you remain functional, he thinks. Now let's get back to work.
As the men go, Patrol Officer Minot looks back over her shoulder -- at the crumbling fortification in the rain, like a rotten tooth rising out of the water...
As the men go, Patrol Officer Minot looks back over her shoulder -- at the crumbling fortification in the snowfall, like a rotten tooth rising out of the water...
As the men go, Patrol Officer Minot looks back over her shoulder -- at the crumbling fortification, like a rotten tooth rising out of the water...
As you take the transceiver in your hands, the lieutenant bites his lower lip, apparently deep in thought.
Back at the station, the lieutenant is standing at a counter, diligently filling out paperwork to hand over to a tired records clerk.
Back off -- *now*! This man has diplomatic immunity. It serves no good to accuse him of a crime he didn't commit.
Be very careful, what information you're gonna share with her.
Beneath that surface query, a touch of self-doubt. The lieutenant isn't sure he can solve the murder *without* you.
But I don't believe it. A child, left unattended, on a warm day. Children make up stories and then end up believing them.
But a case against Evrart would be big...
But he's been drinking, she thinks. This is exactly how he gets out of this every time. It's bad for him, but...
But thank you.
But where is your half-brother, the lieutenant? He should be here...
But, he thinks, it's your call.
But... they don't *want* you back at the station, do they?
C'mon people, try to keep up *any* standards here. It's not about stars, it's... forget it.
C'mon, he thinks -- she's not the lady driver.
Camaraderie even. For a second there he feels less alone -- but then the second passes.
Clearly we have better things to do than sit down and chat with a bunch of drunks!
Collateral damage on the path toward normalcy and progress. Such is your belief, officer.
Come on, this is *not* the way real police solve crimes. The *real* police are some twenty kilometres away, sitting in an armoured motor carriage.
Comrades. The forsaken, the wretched, who tried to rise against the horrors of the world.

Cop with an anger issue, underpaid, overworked and on the verge of collapse in both personal and professional life -- that's more of a rule than an exception. And the lieutenant knows this. He understands.
Cuno must be already on the island, wondering where the hell you are... or maybe he doesn't care. Yeah, he probably doesn't care.
Dammit, ask it already! -- he wants to say.
Dammit, you're a grown man, the lieutenant thinks. Shouldn't have gotten *protective* here.
Damn it -- the lieutenant had the number and he's not here.
Damn it, not this again...
Despite his cool the lieutenant's interested in solving this mystery, too.
Detective Kuuno de Ruyter.
Dirty tactics, officer.
Do not bring it into a gun fight, he thinks. Please understand it's merely ceremonial.
Do the honours, he thinks. Connect it yourself.
Don't ever do that again. But good call, detective.
Don't think it's a good idea to let him speak.
Down below a little doll-sized lieutenant is looking up, shielding his eyes. He's worried for you.
Easy. Walk back from the provocation. They're armed, and they outnumber us. The lieutenant tries to establish eye contact with you.
Eighteen kilometres to the south, in the 41st Precinct's relay booth, a small crowd has gathered around communication officer Jules "Oldboy" Pidieu -- around a dozen cops.
Elsewhere in Revachol, the Lieutenant switches on the radio. His head rocks subtly to a violent track. He needs to hear this story.
Elsewhere in Revachol, the lieutenant has also just turned on the radio. In his privacy, he doesn't even try to stop himself from smiling.

Elsewhere yet, an obese female sits in a wicker chair, her silhouette ball-like against the window. Outside: Grand Couron. The day is turning dim for Sergeant Mack Torson. Hand extended, he approaches. To make sure she *is* dead, more than anything else...
Even now -- you're his colleague.
Even though we're all pigs here, you and Cuno are more pigs than the rest of the pigs. He'll have your back -- only later.
Exactomundo!

Far away, Lieutenant Kitsuragi is flipping through stations in his Kineema. He is not here examining the bullet with you. He should be... he would find this very intriguing. Mention it to him, later when he's here.
Far away, in the darkness of a makeshift morgue behind Station 41's lazareth, Dr. Nix Gottlieb cuts into the cold, dead feet of a murder victim. The veins are oddly black, he suspects a neurotoxin...
Far north -- on the other side of the motorway -- the officer quietly hunches with his hand in the motor carriage.
First he removes the games and puzzles page and sets that aside for later. He never does the crossword during work hours.
Fleeing a crime scene, destroying evidence... Even if Detective Mullen *didn't* commit the murder, he should be facing *years* behind bars.
Focus on one thing, achieve it, then the next... he thinks. That's the task chain.
Follow your brother into death itself.
For example, see how calm I am, although you broke my favourite prybar.
For heaven's sake, he thinks, this has gone too far. Don't make it worse -- just get back to questioning the kid.
For heaven's sake, he thinks, this has gone too far. Don't make this any worse than it is -- just get back to questioning the kid.
For once, I don't seem to know what the right thing to do is.
Foreign dissidents, unwashed criminals, and hoi polloi. Such is your belief, officer.
Fuck it, let 'em have their day, Cuno thinks. Doesn't have to be a joke. Cuno was just sayin' we need to solve this shit...
Get a grip, he thinks. At least do your *personal* stuff when I'm not here.
Get the gun, lieutenant, it's okay. It's safe, he thinks. Just get it.
Go ahead then. Maybe they'll only break his legs.
Go ahead then. Rock her world, he thinks. I'll compose some notes.
Go for it -- the lieutenant has his eyes set on the fat guy. Push Angus. We're almost there.
God I hope he meant it sarcastically. If not, I don't really see how he can continue being a police detective.
God knows I've made my share... he tries not to think of them.
Good. That's what the lieutenant from Precinct 57 would have done as well.
He couldn't be happier to get out of this conversation.
He does not expect you to remain. And why should he?
He feels guilty about failing to protect the inhabitants of the fishing village from a greedy mob boss.
He fires a nod of solidarity in your direction, as if to say: take your time. It's okay.
He is grateful to have that gun back on his belt right now.
He is nervous for your sake, of course, but also his own.
He is not the least bit offended. He just wants you to know his case load should be much higher.
He is your half-brother and you're driving him away. For what?
He is your half-brother.
He means: "Fuck it, let's open the door then."
He means: "You better not be *partying* with this Sylvie, shitkid."
He neither approves, nor disapproves. Yet there is something more there, something you can't put your finger on.
He reads everything on the front page according to the size of the headline. Today, though, he only skims -- none of it seems very interesting.
He seems to be wagging the notebook at you, as though he suspects you may have forgotten why you're here.
He seems to have an unrealistic idea of the RCM's budget and manpower...
He sets down the paper and quickly scribbles the date and time in his notebook, along with a note: 'HDB possible relapse? Check in first thing.'
He sighs and returns to his crossword. He is a police officer, not a baby-sitter.
He thinks -- what are you doing. We almost got him here.
He was. Why else would he call it a very good point? Does this mean you're a *very* good detective? Possibly.
He won't tell you how to do your job, but a good police officer knows when to listen to his partner.
He would follow you into death itself... and you would do the same for him.
He'll tolerate this *personal errand*, but his patience is wearing thin.
He's just glad you're both safe for now.
He's not going to call you Harrier. He'll keep calling you 'officer' when he's angry with you and 'detective' when he's not.
He's right. You can't depend on Kim helping you out again.
He's spoken his peace, he thinks. It's up to you now.
He's thinking -- *are* we done here?
He's thinking the kid has amassed quite the vocabulary of law-enforcement terms. But he's not going to stick his nose into this.
He's thinking: "I like this kind of prodding, whatever the situation. It may lead somewhere yet..."
Heavy-duty case-solving machine.
Here comes the cavalry.
Here the female officer chimes in: "I believe he's part of a certain bureaucrat's security detail." "No fucking way!" The redhead is dumbfounded. "How'd he pull that off?"
His brow furrows while his eyes glaze over. The lieutenant looks regretful for not figuring this out before.
His impatience is feigned. He's actually curious to see how this *personal errand* shakes out.
His impatience is feigned. He's curious to see how this *personal errand* shakes out.
His opinion of you has lowered considerably.

His partner Émile Mollins can't be there -- he's in another apartment with another man who's showing him a dead dog under the radiator. 'It's dead,' Mollins says. "No," the man replies. "I touched him. He's warm."
His words are hiding a tinge of excitement -- he's not so detached from your pursuits after all.
How many micro-bribes would this guy take?
How many years you've been on the force, he's thinking. It'll have that.
However bad it is, pig, we go on, deputy Cuno thinks. We detect. There's nothing more.
Hush, he does not need to be pushed any more. The ball is rolling...
I *really* didn't want to resort to this, the man is thinking.
I am less sceptic about this hunch, he thinks, than I was of the supra-natural premonitions that overtook you at the trash container. He then covers his nose again...
I can't believe I've climbed aboard the piss-train.
I do *not* think that's a good idea.
I don't care about your insane theft, the lieutenant thinks and looks away from the boots. Just know: if anyone asks me, I *will* snitch on you.
I don't need a word-for-word retelling, but we're partners -- say *something*. An adjective will do.
I helped you once. It was your responsibility to help yourself this time. I am sorry.
I hope he means it and at least some kind of an early defence system in his psyche is working.
I mean it, he thinks.
I really do, the lieutenant thinks -- is she thrown off, yet? He's looking at the woman, assessing her...
I really have, he thinks. She was strong. She made it easy for us.
I see what you're doing there. Let's push him.
I smell pseudoscience, he's thinking. Not a big fan of that.
I understand -- we've all been through similar things. It can be overcome.
I was 80% sure the gun is empty and *still* the instinct almost took over.
I was 95% sure the gun is empty and *still* that instinctual muscle twitch could have ended in an unnecessary death.
I'll wait. I'm on standby, he thinks, his hands sweaty around the machine.
I'm actually not, he thinks. I just dislike delinquents.
I'm beginning to suspect that you might indeed be completely adrift in this reality, thinks the lieutenant. How can it be *that* bad? Never mind -- we're in this now.
I'm not expecting him to get far with this, the lieutenant consoles himself.
I'm not surprised children have seen you running around with *party eyes* on, he thinks. Not at all.
I'm not surprised it's already getting out of hand.
I'm not telling you twice.
I'm put off by this religious stuff, he thinks -- and maybe the ceiling-climbing, too. It's all very hard to square with the lieutenant's own view of reality.
I'm really sorry.
I've softened him up. As best as I could. Now it's on you to finish the job.

If an assault were launched on this building right now -- if the windows came crashing down and the whole world descended upon you -- this man would hurl himself in death's way to save you. You are sure of this -- but why?
If only Kim were here right now! You could ask him if he sees a case here. The sight of those bullet holes stirs something in you.
If only Lieutenant Kitsuragi were here to help you piece together the evidence.
If only Lieutenant Kitsuragi were here to make sense of it...
If the lieutenant were here he would have an idea what to do. But he isn't. He's somewhere filling paperwork. Looks like you'll just have to work it out on your own.
If the lieutenant were here, he would remind you that you don't speak only for yourself but the entire RCM. What you say now will reflect on all of your half-brothers and sisters.
If you think it's important -- you *have* been right before.
In a ditch off a road below the 8/81, he thinks. A young father. Then he shakes his head, to make the memory stop.
In a mould-covered bathroom inside a trashed hostel near the edge of Martinaise, a middle-aged man grips the sides of a cold sink in white-knuckled fury...

In a small office behind the Old Military Hospital, hunched under the green glow of his desk light, officer Hans Blau browses through a test print. Of his futurist magazine. It's called "The Futurist". The typeface on the header is too small...
In my dream, he thinks, he said I've failed his children... you glance at the lieutenant. Then back to her.

In the break room of Precinct 41, Sergeant Mack Torson is just now warming a grey sausage over an electric range. The oil in the pan hisses and pops. Torson is dabbing a spot off his pants when his partner, Satellite-Officer Chester McLaine enters, sniffs the air, and frowns...
It actually sounds extremely bad, but let's not give him the satisfaction.
It certainly won't improve your precinct's image within the community.
It evokes a sense of recognition and fellowship among those privileged enough to understand.
It feels like you haven't worn yours in a long-long time.
It feels safe to know that the lieutenant's got your back, now and always.
It is a mark of the lieutenant's respect for you that he does not press you on this decidedly *extra-curricular* errand.
It is a very useful skill to have, he thinks. For all sorts of life-activities. Like reading, and...
It is not my flag, he thinks -- my flag is the signal blue of the Zone of Control.
It remains a mystery what you mean by this *something close*. This isn't about you. It's about *reality*.
It's difficult to say what the lieutenant thinks of this historic apology. His face does not belie emotions.

It's early spring and the rains are coming. An officer enters a low hut of stone and wood. Inside, weapons are piled against the walls. Rifles with splintering stocks... and swords. Tens if not hundreds.
It's good we discussed your history with her, he thinks. I can play along.
It's hard to say. It's been so long since you wore yours.
It's meant in earnest. Please don't mistake it for a jab.
It's more than that. He just can't. One final time...
It's no wonder you are like you are, he thinks. But then again...
It's not meant as nagging, just an observation.
It's not my style, he thinks, glancing at the man in sunglasses. And the woman beside him. Ooo boy, they're mad at him. No wonder. He just doesn't recognize them.
It's okay. Women, racism, turning back time -- the lieutenant wouldn't be interested anyway.
It's the only home you have now. But you can't go back. Not like this.
It's true in more ways than you know.
It's your refusal to take responsibility I don't like.
Just don't *mix* it with anything, he thinks, looking you over. Thankfully his sense of balance seems okay. Whatever. I'm not getting involved.
Just please ask them normal questions.
Just trust me and follow my lead.
Just trust me and go for the gun.
Kim Kitsuragi is a nice guy. Wish he was here right now.
Lay off her.
Leeches, fleas, and thieves. All the freeloader scum living off the hard work of others. Such is your belief.
Lest we leave riddled with bullet holes. This animosity is ancient.
Let's *try* not to worry, he thinks.
Let's do this tango, pig, he thinks. Detective Pig and detective Cunn on the case. We bouncin'.
Lieutenant Kitsuragi does not appreciate you withholding case specific information.
Lieutenant Kitsuragi lies in the hospital, recovering. Barely. Are you really done here, he would ask you? Are you done with the investigation?
Lieutenant Kitsuragi stands in front of a mirror, fixing his black bomber jacket. You should be asking him that question.
Lieutenant Kitsuragi stands in front of a mirror, fixing his orange bomber jacket. You should be asking him that question.
Lieutenant Kitsuragi would have a prybar in his Kineema, but he's not here right now.
Lieutenant Kitsuragi would probably know what to do, but right now you're just gonna have to leave her like this.
Looking at him now, with both hands on his headphones, you see very briefly what the lieutenant must have been like when he first joined the RCM...
Looks like the lieutenant has a plan. Let him do this.
Man down! A trooper has been left behind the enemy lines!
Maybe even the love of your friends and colleagues?

Meanwhile, his colleague is filling in the last clues of his nightly crossword when he hears what seems to be some kind of animalistic howling from the adjoining room. He directs his eyes toward the door of their shared bathroom...

Meanwhile, in the antechamber of the Station 57 lazareth, a neatly packed pair of cargo pants and a black bomber jacket sit in a locker. Nested on the jacket -- a small block of milled aluminium, tucked away in a leather case.

Meanwhile, in the antechamber of the Station 57 lazareth, a neatly packed pair of cargo pants and an orange bomber jacket sit in a locker. Nested on the jacket -- a small block of milled aluminium, tucked away in a leather case.
Meanwhile, the lieutenant is sitting behind his desk, hunched over a stack of paperwork. His help would be invaluable right now.
Morrand's from Traffic.
Needless to say, it violates nearly every RCM regulation for a detective to investigate a murder in which he is a suspect.
Nervous for who, though, you cannot say.

Night. Captain Ptolemy Pryce steps into the yard. A piebald horse waits by the motor carriage, chewing oats out of an oat-bag. Seagulls fly overhead. The sky is black. Captain Pryce wears a black suit and a standard patrol coat as he mounts the horse to head home...
Nix Gottlieb, station medic, throws a bag containing a small bullet into the medical waste disposer. Then he pops some caramel candy into his mouth and says: "Next." There's a line behind his door...
No -- he already is a sergeant. That's what you're feeling. He just wasn't sure what *you* are.
No one knows what to say. But they agree. Keeping you on the force endangers civilians.
No, he's not. He's going keep calling you "officer" when he's angry with you -- and "detective" when he's not.
No. The rest will stay. They will hold their ground. Even if it means dying here -- with you.
No. You *can* see it. This young man has junior officer material in him. In another life, where he trusts you.
Not a lot of moral fibre on this one yet, he thinks. Wriggled out of it.
Not a soul.

Not far from here a 34-year-old man called Jean Vicquemare reluctantly picks his facial hair while a dark-haired woman in an officer's suit waits for an answer. A typical police station morning hums in the background: telephones ringing, the whirr of a roll'o'dex...
Not in a million years.
Not just granite -- tightly packed RCM sergeant material.
Not only his name, but his address... You are a goddamn menace.

Not so fast. Somewhere between the GRIH and Le Cycle, the good lieutenant steps out of his Kineema. A brisk, chill wind greets him. You get the feeling he should be here for this. Listen when he's here.
Not so fast. The lieutenant is out there somewhere. Doing something. You get the feeling he should be here for this. Listen when he's here.
Not this, he seems to say. Anything but this.
Nota bene: The lieutenant is not at all convinced that anything will be revealed.
Nothing to do here without the lieutenant... You must return here with him.
Of course, one doesn't need to be a communist to be attuned to the thoughts of your brothers and sisters.
Of course, you don't have to. You can talk about anything you want.
Of course. Yes. It's preposterous.
Oh, headless brother, where art thou?
Okay, the man with the sunglasses and his *hypothetical Station 41*. Weird, right?
Okay. Fine.
Okay. I was clearly wrong. He is a firefighter, male nurse, animal control agent -- something of that kind. Not a cop. Go on with your cop work. Don't let me stop you again.
On his right hand side -- a little blue notebook. Open at case notes...
On the bridge, Officers Torson and McLaine are standing guard. The muscular Torson wears jeans and a leather jacket. When Vicquemare passes him, the young man remarks...
On the bridge, Officers Torson and McLaine are standing guard. Torson wears jeans and a fishnet wifebeater. Satellite-Officer Vicquemare passes by, and the young man remarks to him...
On the other hand... it's not that I'm *not* interested in abandoned radiocomputers.
One more down.
One more loose end down. We're doing this, detective.
One needs tending to right now, he thinks, looking around the room. God, these apes...
One of the men, trying not to slip on old newspapers, makes his way to the window and pulls the curtains apart. Roaches scatter as the light comes in.
Ordinary people -- familiar, each and every one of them.
Painful images replay in the lieutenant's mind as he lies still in his sick bed: gun fumes, the flash of metal, and occasionally a clumsy stack of scaffolding...
Part of him is amazed you're both able to just *dance* after all these horrifying events in Martinaise.
Passing along frivolous inter-departmental stereotypes is not usually his *oeuvre*. He regrets bringing it up.
Patrol Officer Judit Minot points west. "The fishing village..." She glances at her watch. "We meet in 15 minutes. It's a ten minute walk."
Patrol Officer Judit Minot stands beneath the awning overhanging the entrance, struggling to open her big black umbrella.
Patrol Officer Judit Minot stands in the corner, beneath the narrow awning that hangs over the main entrance. She is watching the snow absent-mindedly as she searches for her gloves in her briefcase.
Perhaps I shouldn't have tried, he thinks.
Perhaps it was an *unspoken* agreement between you and the junior officer?
Perhaps someday the lieutenant will tell you about some of the decisions that won't leave him alone, but it won't be this day.
Pig's got mad timing. Fast-twitch fiber shit, interceptor shit. Animal instinct-style.
Please attend to personal hygiene on your own time.
Please get yourself together, the lieutenant from Precinct 57 thinks.
Please, please do not melt down again. We're so close to wrapping this up and getting out of here.
Police colours.

Porta Rosa, a side alley of the Boogie Street spearhead. A young man in his early twenties approaches Patrol Officer Émile Mollins and asks for a cigarette. As Officer Mollins reaches in his coat pocket for the pack of 'Astra' he just purchased this morning...
Precinct 57th's finest scans the room, leaving the speaking to you: He trusts you -- maybe against his better judgement, but he does.
Probably shouldn't have asked you this.
Relax, he thinks. There's no one in there. If we can't open it, neither can anyone else.

Rows of houses on either side, hunching over the sidewalks -- and Precinct 41 with its dome roof growing distant. Around him the streets are silent. A kid on the corner waves at the captain as he takes the turn on Perdition and Main. The horse neighs. The captain nods back.
Satellite-Officer Jean Vicquemare rushes down the Precinct stairs, umbrella in hand. It's unopened. He doesn't seem pleased about the spring rain.
Satellite-Officer Jean Vicquemare rushes down the station stairs, angrily hunched over in the falling slush.
Satellite-Officer Vicquemare looks out of the window grimly, then puts his coffee down and turns to Patrol Officer Minot. "We can either take a room here in the Whirling or go home for today."
Should I contact his station's lazareth? Behind his back?
Should we ever face a totally unrealistic scenario like this, you would make a much more convincing hooker, *officer*.
Sincere worry, officer to officer. He feels you need help and regrets that he doesn't know how to offer it.
Sitting at a desk, Lieutenant Kitsuragi fiddles with a pen, then writes something on the paper in front of him. He shouldn't be doing that, he should be here discussing the footprints.
Six kilometres southwest -- in the Valley of Dogs -- junior officer Chad Tillbrook takes aim at a rabid, black dog licking its wounds in the grass.
Sleep is what he needs most right now.
So Union people think he was a killer, he thinks -- even Sleepy here? This doesn't help a *lot*, but it's something. Good work, detective.
Something here is eating at the lieutenant, as much as he would like to move past it.
Sometimes police work is about human dignity -- about giving back names to anonymous victims.

Somewhere -- not here -- the helpful Lieutenant Kitsuragi is making notes in his blue notebook. He tilts it ever so slightly, a glowing rectangle catches the light. He could advise you with this. The next moment...

Somewhere between the GRIH and Le Cycle, the good lieutenant steps out of his Kineema. A brisk, chill wind greets him. You get the feeling he should have been here for this. Listen again when he's here.
Somewhere in Central Jamrock, Lieutenant Kitsuragi steps into the public library, rows of shelves pass on either side... you feel he should be here for in-depth flower analysis.

Somewhere in Jamrock North, a small wood shed behind Rozenkrantz Row, Lieutenant Nick Feuerbach puts his hand to the chest of a small corpse, no larger than a monkey. It's raining outside, light drizzle. There is darkness in the shed.
Somewhere in Martinaise, Lieutenant Kitsuragi is consulting his notebook... A sudden draught turns a page between his hands. You're going to need him if you want to listen to the wind.
Somewhere in a dank rental apartment, two police officers stand in the dark. Trash and liquor bottles cover the floor. Things are not good.
Somewhere in a hospital bed the lieutenant turns onto his side, feverish and exhausted...
Somewhere in a station lazareth lieutenant-detective Kitsuragi turns on the side and says: ouch. The wounds are agonizing.

Somewhere in an office lit by a single green desk lamp captain Ptolemaios Pryce -- 58, bald and bespectacled -- is writing in a ledger on his desk. Rows and rows of days and weeks, laconic remarks in a single column: *patrol*, *case*, *vacation*, *injured*...

Somewhere not far from here the lieutenant is preparing her file. "Klaasje Amandou," he writes. You realize the detective should be here for what comes next. It could prove... *dangerous* to go in alone.
Somewhere not so distant, officer Chad Tillbrook refuses a bribe from a local hood. He doesn't know you've just made his job, and the job of all honest cops, a little bit harder.

Somewhere on a hidden field, a flash of light, a whirring sound. Camera lenses focus on a man in a ragged police uniform standing in a field of blood-red poppies. A blurry figure dressed in white stands right by the camera...

Somewhere under the curved roof of a former silk factory, shaped like a ladybird with two chimneys, Police Captain Ptolemy Pryce sits behind a heavy wooden desk. Resident medic Nix Gottlieb pours him coffee. It's silent in the captain's office...
Somewhere, Lieutenant Kitsuragi is preparing a report. He should be here for this, you realise. You have to return later.

Special consultant  Heidelstam is optimistic: "We'll see the boat when he comes. Let's go get a coffee until then. I know this interesting little place, where..." His voice trails off as the three walk down the jetty.

Station 41. A man carries a crate of rusted, unused badges. "You think these would net us something at the annual auction?" A balding detective drags a comb futilely across his head. "Shit, who'd want those? Just dump them into the river."
Still caught up in this crossfire, too...
Still, against his better judgement, this made him think just a little bit less of you
Strangely enough, it's not pity. He would never pity a fellow officer. He truly appears to think you've solved your fair share of cases -- perhaps more than him.
Suddenly he's a little worried about your memory...
Suddenly, you recall: Two officers must be present for a field autopsy. You take your hand from the dead man's face and open your eyes. You will still need to come back with the lieutenant.
Superior? But he's not in the command chain...
Sure, he thinks. It's about *transparency*.
Take care of the people who protect the people.
Thank you. Although...
Thank you.
That makes absolutely no sense, but I really don't want to get into it with you.
That reminds you, where has your half-brother gone?
That would be a first -- or a fourth -- but who's counting, he thinks. Very rarely does anyone actually get framed.
That's between you and them, he thinks.
That's cop for: "I haven't offered to pay because I don't have any money either."
That's it. Motive. We have it.
That's it. The cavalry has arrived.
That's not good. Trust among officers is extremely important. If you don't improve your rapport, there could be consequences down the line...
That's right. These two young men naturally defer to your aura of authority, which you nevertheless wear as lightly as your halogen watermarks.
The bald man squints at a file, then returns it to the cabinet. A new drawer opens with a grinding screech. He turns back to the redhead in the doorway. "What made you think of him now?"
The bedraggled cop is holding a manure fork. "Don't hurt me," he whimpers.

The clatter of typewriter keys fills the main hall of a re-appropriated Silk Mill -- Precinct 41. Chad Tillbrook presses ENTER. Outside: Officer *Elfboy* Williams slams the door of an armoured motor carriage...
The cop radio is buzzing on silent...

The coroner and his assistant are wrapped in blue: hands covered in blue gloves, mouths covered by blue masks, bodies by blue aprons. The coroner wipes his brow, scalpel still in his hand. "Get the light. Shine it in his mouth," he tells his assistant. The coroner squats, to better see as the light illumines the darkness inside. "Well, shit. Can't blame them for missing that. Get the saw, Alan. This just got fun."
The fading marks are too degraded to draw any forensic conclusions. Just chips in the sandstone.
The lieutenant adjusts his electronic wristwatch. Psychological and physical training from an unknown government body. I noticed it. You noticed it. They haven't.
The lieutenant does not approve of this. At all.
The lieutenant doesn't like this. It may be less destructive to your working relationship to just come back alone later.
The lieutenant is a police officer of the *old school*. His concerns are material and extrinsic.
The lieutenant is concerned about the lost gun and feels that the fact you haven't prioritized looking for it is unfortunate, if inevitable, and doesn't put the RCM in a good light.
The lieutenant is genuinely concerned. This is not a situation he ever thought he'd find himself in.
The lieutenant is lightly amused by the situation.
The lieutenant is not in his room. He's found a little 24-hour cafe southeast of the roundabout. He's taking a break and reading the newspaper, after his usual fashion.
The lieutenant is not pleased with this turn of events, but it's unlikely that he'll move to intervene to stop them.
The lieutenant is out there somewhere. Doing something. You get the feeling he should have been here for this. Listen again when he's here.
The lieutenant is practised in the art of putting on a show for one's superiors.

The lieutenant is sitting on the edge of his bed, twenty kilometres from here, and cleaning his boots. An archetypal policeman like him would *definitely* come up with some questions to ask right now. Unlike you, standing here alone, unable to gather your thoughts.
The lieutenant lies in recovery. Where he does so, however, is unknown.
The lieutenant should *clearly* be here for this. You're lost.
The lieutenant stands behind a white table, cleaning his gun. It would truly come in handy in a tense situation like this one.
The lieutenant watches the boys take their jackets off -- with mild amusement.
The lieutenant will be more lenient toward sharing those, hopefully.
The lieutenant would probably agree that, in this situation, it's best to be thorough.
The lieutenant, elsewhere in Revachol, runs a finger over the rough mesh of a stereo. He is deep in thought. He needs to be here for this.
The lieutenant, on your left, is unusually lenient toward them.

The man shoots him point blank in his chest. Breathless, the patrol officer collapses in the gutter. His right hand is grabbing the armour on his chest. The bullet didn't pierce it, but he can't breathe. On the pavement, the patter of the perpetrator's feet growing distant.
The man's a prosecutor's nightmare. Solving a murder counts for nothing if all the evidence gets thrown out in court over police misconduct.
The minuscule amount of amphetamine doesn't interest the lieutenant in the slightest. He listens instead to something in the other room.
The morning will never come.
The next time you make statements like this I suggest you first change into something a bit less *promiscuous* than those sleazy robes, officer.
The officers go, leaving behind the writing -- still smouldering. ONE DAY, it says, I WILL RETURN TO YOUR SIDE.
The officers go, leaving behind the writing, still smelling of petroleum. ONE DAY, it says, I WILL RETURN TO YOUR SIDE.
The one you seem so set on driving away, the lieutenant, is a natural place to start.
The police of animals, yes!
The price of a 1.5 litre bottle of Commodore Red? The first line of the Firefighter's Code? Square root of three? I don't know what you mean by *something close to you*. Ask a normal question.
The probe and the case *converged*, he thinks. This was quite the find.
The sight of bullet holes stirs something in you, making you forget the lieutenant's surname.
The small room is filled with cigarette smoke, abuzz with laughter, when Officer Judit Minot enters. Her left arm is in bandages and her hair trimmed short.
The weight is reassuring. Like a crenel on solid fortification. Pat pat pat...
The woman says to him: "Good to see you here, special consultant. We're worried about the lieutenant-yefreitor."
Then don't. No one's forcing you.
Then he turns to you. He gives you a half smile along with an almost imperceptible nod of encouragement.
Then he turns to you. He shakes his head slightly and looks back at the amplifier.
Then he turns to you. His expression seems to say, 'You'd better not leave this mess for me alone'.
There -- motive, detective. We have it.
There are officers who wear the signature Perseus Black uniforms to the highest ranks in the RCM and end up buried in them as well -- others do it more *casually*. Looks like you're one of them.
There could be weapons aiming at us right now -- somewhere above, in the buildings. The other merc. Don't push this -- he's thinking -- this is not the time.
There is *plenty* here to work with once he's in custody -- and the lieutenant knows it. He gives you a little nod to proceed.

There is a little slip of questions that keeps everything in *order*. The questions are inside a blue notebook. The notebook is inside the lieutenant's pocket. Before the questions leave your mouth, you realize he should be here for this.
There is absolutely no need to push this any further.
There is little time for this...
There's more going on here than we know.
There's no point in pushing it further, he thinks. This is already a victory. We'll learn more about this eighth Hardie sooner or later.
There's no time to dwell on this moment. You've got to keep moving...
There's nothing in the world we need to talk about less than my glasses.
There's nothing to add, he thinks. The situation speaks for itself.
There's something that binds you to him. Some kind of an outfit maybe...? A uniform!
There's something we're missing here... something you can't put our finger on. You know what -- just ask him. I know it sounds crazy (and you'll probably get laughed at). But still...

There, on a dilapidated jetty in a nameless village, two police officers and one special consultant look across a narrow strip of sea. The ruins of a seafort stick out of the water, built by Filippe II, re-appropriated by the Commune, then lost in the Landing...
These machines sometimes harbour traps, he thinks -- alarm systems and the like. Let's be careful.
They are veterans of the same war. Somewhere west, in Coal City his enemy is lying in a dark room, on a metal slider, not breathing. Like he will too. Soon. Half of the war between these men is over.

They are veterans of the same war. Somewhere west, in Coal City his enemy is lying in a dark room, on a metal slider. In L'Ossuaire Municipal, like he will too. Soon. Half of the war between these men is over.
They speak of change. The city. The tension on the streets. They speak of the events of April and the blood on the streets in May.
They're huddled close, in a formation. Steel. The rest will stay. Even if it means dying here -- with him.
They're not shitting you, detective. This is what the writers think passes for police procedure.
They're your posse. Or what remains of it. Hand-picked. Hand-lost.
This *soldier* is the highlight of the day for me, he thinks. This little stick right here.
This Lena is wacky enough for the motley crew. Hire her on the spot!

This feels like something you should return to with Lieutenant Kitsuragi present. Also, it makes you feel like an idiot for not having an evidence bag on you -- what will you do with a dead man's trousers?
This habit of dispensing wisdom has been brought to his attention before. He will not think less of you for lashing out -- he is trying to curb it.
This has got to be the most.... wow...
This has lowered his opinion of you -- but only a little.
This is Precinct 41 -- as close as you have to home now. It's far away...
This is a fun challenge for her, an opportunity to contribute beyond doing her job by rote. She'll gladly put in the extra effort for team RCM!
This is tangential at best, but the lieutenant's *detective instinct* is still active.
This is the orderly pattern of duty; a picture of a job well done, over and over.
This man does not crack under pressure. Stars would die before he'd succumb to any kind of manipulation. With this realization comes a sudden flash of pride.
This man is your half brother. You feel it. But *why*?
This may be the only way, he thinks. I won't hold it against you. In fact, we  probably *should* reconsider later.
This should cheer you up.
This was your responsibility. I cannot help you now. I am sorry.
This whole thing was a detour -- and a fatal one, he thinks.

This would also be a good time to finally *name* the case -- if only Lieutenant Kitsuragi were here so you could coordinate. No use naming it without him... maybe at a later time you can come back to this?
Those triangle patches on his vest... you have a feeling they *mean* something. Like they're similar to the halogen rectangle on your jacket.
Though you haven't exactly been a credit to the investigation so far, the lieutenant doesn't want to be left in the cold, either.

Three figures on the spring ice. One, a shortish man, is standing with his shoulders slightly hunched. Another, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the third, a middle-aged man with bloated cheeks, jumping up and down on the sea ice...

Three figures on the spring ice. One, a shortish man, is standing with his shoulders slightly hunched. Another, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the third, a middle-aged man with bloated cheeks, who's screaming at her with all his might...

Three figures on the spring ice. One, a shortish man, is standing with his shoulders slightly hunched. Another, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the third, a middle-aged man with spectacular sideburns, jumping up and down on the sea ice...

Three figures on the spring ice. One, a shortish man, is standing with his shoulders slightly hunched. Another, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the third, a middle-aged man with spectacular sideburns, who's screaming at her with all his might...
Three-hundred metres inland special consultant Trant Heidelstam cups his ear. Satellite-Officer Vicquemare nods to him on the jetty: "I heard it."
Three-hundred metres inland, on the jetty, special consultant Trant Heidelstam cups his ear. Satellite-Officer Vicquemare nods to him: "I heard it."
Titus -- behind you -- must be aiming at him right now. Don't forget, there's additional reinforcements. Just survive this...
To his left, his partner Émile Mollins whispers: "You heard what happened to Tequila Sunset? In Martinaise?"
To your left, the lieutenant considers your actions -- with some puzzlement.
To your right you see the lieutenant give you a quick sideways glance...

Twenty-eight kilometres to the northeast, in the infirmary of Precinct 57, Lieutenant Kim 'Pinball' Kitsuragi rests under a heart monitor. A triage sign coloured yellow hangs from his injury record -- safe, it means. He coughs, propped up against the pillow.
Two figures on the spring ice. One, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the other, a middle-aged man with bloated cheeks, jumping up and down on the sea ice...
Two figures on the spring ice. One, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the other, a middle-aged man with bloated cheeks, who's screaming at her with all his might...
Two figures on the spring ice. One, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the other, a middle-aged man with spectacular sideburns, jumping up and down on the sea ice...
Two figures on the spring ice. One, a young woman, tries her best to ignore the other, a middle-aged man with spectacular sideburns, who's screaming at her with all his might...

Two men and a woman stand on the beach, under a row of houses long abandoned to war damage. The words "Feld Electrical" have peeled off the turn-of-the-century waterfront development. One of the men carries a small boy on his shoulders. The boy looks up at the faded logo.
Two men and a woman stand on the concrete square of a nameless village, looking at a small yellow boat as it draws closer. The sea is calm.
Two officers of the law against the world. Nodding in unison. It is your source of power. The bond of camaraderie between you... is palpable...
Two police officers step out of the Whirling-in-Rags cafeteria. Satellite-Officer Jean Vicquemare inspects a series of burnt black letters splashed across the plaza mosaic.
Two police officers step out of the Whirling-in-Rags cafeteria. Satellite-Officer Jean Vicquemare inspects giant letters across the plaza mosaic, in dark red government-marked heavy fuel oil.
Uh... I want to say... yes? I think.

Under Precinct 41, where typewriters fire long into the night, and officers walk the great steps and the bridge. Long after the card has landed in the cold shore waters, its writing dissolved. Material disintegrated.
Unimportant?
Utter madness, he thinks -- as a compliment.
Wanna hear what's stupid? Somewhere in a one room apartment on Boogie Street a young man shows Patrol Officer Tillbrook his genital warts, asking if they're *cancer*.
Was it a mistake leaving him alone with that guy?
We almost fell for it, he thinks. *Almost*.
We didn't fall for it, he thinks. There's pride in there.
We had to have *something*. His foot-tapping has become less patient now.
We missed it, he thinks. We damn missed a spot. We should have... then the lieutenant collects himself.
We should have discussed your history before we came here, he thinks. So many unpleasant surprises with this guy...
We should've factored this in. Damn it.
We'll find it again, man. We'll get it, junior officer Cuno thinks. We'll fuckin' nail that phasmid one day.
We'll need more if we're going to pin this one down, he thinks -- good questions, though. Sharp eyes, officer.
We're all under stress. This is turning into a great big mess. I'm not judging. Just keep it together.
Well, *that* testimony won't be admissible any longer.
Were you there?
What about the lieutenant? He's not here, but you might want him to join...
What about the lieutenant?
What are you doing, he's thinking. We almost got him here.
What are you doing?
What are you up to now?
What he means is, he can't believe you're about to renounce your half-brothers and sisters.
What he means is: I don't believe you actually think you did it for all mankind. But whatever keeps you in high spirits is okay by him.
What he means is: I don't care if you have a corrupt scheme, almost everyone does. Whatever keeps you sober and concentrated on work.
What he means is: You can't possibly have handed them this real estate for such a flimsy reason -- then again, whatever keeps your nose out of the bottle and on the job.
What he means is: You're right, actually. The tent was a safety hazard and this place was deserted anyway.
What he means is: You're right. I do not understand. Do whatever you have to do to keep your nose out of the bottle.
Whatever he has in store for you, it can't be good, he thinks. I'll do my best.
Whatever you had planned, you have to do it when the lieutenant's gone.
When I said *be wacky* I didn't mean *wildly, grossly irresponsible and damaging to the RCM*.
When he's finished with the front page, he scans the local box scores. The Stormers lost again, 17-32. He shakes his head and checks his watch. It's time to get back to it...
When you clocked out, *I* became responsible for your cases -- and your special task force.
Where in the GRIH is the lieutenant? Possibly speeding from one terminal to another in glorious solitude? Wherever he is, you need him -- your other questions will make little sense without him here.
While Evrart is distracted by your odd behaviour, the lieutenant's eyes are mapping everything around you. The folder, desk, papers on the wall...
With an agitated gait Satellite-Officer Jean-Heron Vicquemare paces the jetty, 22 kilometres East -- in Martinaise: "What could he *possibly* be doing there for so long," he says.
With worry in his eyes. He does not know if it was the right thing to do. But he doesn't say anything.
Women, racism, turning back time -- it couldn't interest him less.
Words are not necessary to feel the lieutenant's discontent for the situation.
Words are one thing, but he's not letting you follow through with it.
Would fit in? Am crazy enough? Can take the stress? He doesn't know how to finish the sentence.
Yes, of course -- preposterous. I mean, you would *remember* if they were, right? Who forgets their squadmates? That's not possible.
Yes. Just cross it off the list. It's probably not true though.
Yes. Nurses, why not...
You *are* making excuses, he thinks. For aiming your gun at a child nonetheless.
You *know* what it is. It's like the two of you know each other. Just ask him.

You almost say the words, then stop -- the lieutenant is off somewhere, 28 kilometres from here, probably reading the *manual* for incarceration. The one he *knows how to use*. Come back and say this with Kim here; you can't handle it without him.
You always get to be the crazy one, right? Fine, then.
You and him are on the same page now -- about The Law and being it. This has affected your relationship positively.
You are part of a large family. You are never alone...
You could swear it's more of an instinct than anything else, but you *know* it's a cop's cloak.
You crossed a line. This will not be forgotten.
You don't have to be a hero.
You feel fortified by his assurance. It's going to be all right.
You have no business with those boots. But here we are.
You haven't been drinking, she thinks. So maybe this time...
You haven't. But it's not too late. You share a bond that's thicker than blood.
You really ought to open this door. It's dangerous to go ahead alone.
You should have heeded your partner's warning. Now it's too late.
You should look at the badge when Lieutenant Kitsuragi is around -- he could help you make sense of all this.
You should probably check on Kim. It doesn't sound like he's doing all too well.
You should probably check on Kim. That's what good partners do.
You should share your findings with him as soon as possible.
You should show all this to the lieutenant, see what he makes of it.
You shouldn't attempt to climb this ladder without the lieutenant here. What if you fall and need to be rushed to the hospital?
You shouldn't attempt to get to the roof without the lieutenant here. What if you fall and need to be rushed to the hospital?
You stand there with the ledger open like a lonesome idiot, realizing you have no idea how to conduct an autopsy without the lieutenant, who is out there doing something immeasurably serious.
You think he's in his room, but do you really know? No, you don't. You only know that he isn't here...
You used to believe you came from a large family with many half-brothers and sisters, but where are they now? However you shout, they can't hear you...
You will have to deal with it too, the lieutenant thinks. This is why I didn't want you to know.
You'd better be going somewhere case-related with this.
You'll need the lieutenant's help to carry her over.
You're going to have to figure this one out on your own.
You're not only speaking for yourself, he means to tell you, you're speaking for all of the RCM. What you say now will reflect on all of your half-brothers and sisters.
You've definitely lost the love of your colleagues.
You, your friends, your family. Everyone you had ever known. This was *you*.
Your brothers have left you.
Your half-brother is proud to stand beside you.
Your half-brother, the lieutenant, is a natural place to start.
Your partner needs backup. Now's your moment to shine!

"...and so he removed my appendix *on the spot*, while the party was still on in the other room. And I was delirious with pain and all the schnapps I'd had, but I remember thinking -- man, that Gottlieb can stitch..."

"And then that motherfucker Gottlieb -- reeking of schnapps -- ordered me into his butcher's room..." Officer Fischer is in a storytelling mood -- he continues: "And I ask him, 'You sure, doc?' and he's cool as can be, 'Yeah, if you're in pain, we've gotta get that baby out...'"
"Back to that shithole," he says.
"Bleed, pig!" someone opens a window and says, but Émile can't see who. His sight grows dim with pain...
"But why... why would I hurt my own *peone*?" asks the man in white. "What kind of an *animal* would do such thing?"
"Don't know. He just kinda popped into my mind." "Ahem, excuse me, Chester," says a female officer who threads her way into the file room. "Where is old Ace now?" the bald officer asks.
"Family connections, I'd imagine? His father-in-law works for the Office of Standards and Best Practices." With that, the woman exits, a grey file folder tucked under her arm.

"Goddamn..." officer Michel 'Elfboy' Williams speaks to himself. His partner, Sundance Fischer, looks at the patrol uniform he's wearing -- then at an identical suit framed on the wall. It's blue and covered in dust. "Let's get the fuck out of here," he turns to Williams, "he hasn't been here in days."
"Have you seen the way he dresses?" The man asks. "It's like he's not even a cop anymore. Shows up in here looking like a clown, reeking of alcohol. Screams at everyone... I'm done with that guy."
"He must be *really* out of it, yelling like that. Well, at least he's *alive*...." Officer Minot in the blue uniform frowns: "Jean, maybe something *happened*? He's in distress..."
"He's there, doing... *what* exactly I don't know," Satellite-Officer Vicquemare points at the ruins. "Behind that anti-aircraft-something. That's why we can't see him."
"He's warm," Mollins replies, "because he's under the radiator."

"Honestly, babe," says John McCoy, crossing his ankles over said desk, "I don't feel anything anymore. It's just like brushing my teeth -- I do it once or twice a week and don't really think about it." There's no trace of guilt in his voice.
"Let him sort it out for himself."
"Let's go home, Jean. Nothing's going to happen today," she responds quietly. Jean takes his blond wig off. "Call Heidelstam. He can give us a ride."

"Only a month later, when I got a chance to properly thank him, did I find out that *he* had no recollection of the event -- that's Gottlieb for ya. In league with some devil, he is, I'm sure of it. Cheers!" The clinking of beer glasses is drowned in group conversation...
"Policework?" Patrol Officer Judit Minot replies, with a tinge of hope in her voice. A tinge of warmth too, against the cold air rising from the water...

"Really?" Nix Gottlieb looks up from the list. "I hear he's quite unstable."
"You say that like it's a *bad* thing," Captain Ptolemy Pryce points his pen at the doctor. It's dim in his office and the curtains are drawn. "Harry's our man, he'll pull through -- and when he does he'll side with Revachol."
"Understood." Gottlieb returns to the list. "Minot?"
"Of course."

"Really?" Nix Gottlieb looks up from the list. "I hear he's quite unstable."
"You say that like it's a *bad* thing," Captain Ptolemy Pryce points his pen at the doctor. It's dim in his office and the curtains are drawn. "Harry's our man, he'll pull through -- and when he does, he'll side with Revachol."
"Understood." Gottlieb returns to the list. "Minot?"
"Of course."
"Remember that guy, Ace Ulsterberg?" the redheaded officer asks. He leans against the doorframe while his bald companion is hunched over an open filing drawer...
"The children carry them up. Come May, the streets will be flooded..." Outside the wind rattles the loose hatches. "*Flooded* with cheap weapons. In angry hands."

"The guy who got canned for trying to arrest that diplomat?" the bald man asks. "Well, *technically* he got canned for being shit-faced on the job. But yeah, trying to arrest the ambassador from Gottwald *while* shit-faced was probably a contributing factor..."
"Then why are you wasting time here?" coughs Kim. "He's out there. In Martinaise. Unsupervised, *without cover*. You need to get there..." His cough gets the best of him, he grabs a glass.
"They're antiques," says Lieutenant John 'The Archetype' McCoy to his partner. "They're digging them up from the catacombs now, fixing them. Old caches from the Revolution. "
"To inquire about the Lieutenant-Yefreitor, sir. I am his partner," says Satellite-Officer Jean Vicquemare, standing in the doorway.

"Torson?"
"Yes."
"McLaine?"
"Yes."
"Heidelstam?"
"No."
"Vicquemare?"
"Yes."
"Du Bois?"
"Of course."

"Torson?"
"Yes."
"McLaine?"
"Yes."
"Heidelstam?"
"No."
"Vicquemare?"
"Yes."
"Du Bois?"
"Yes."
"We always do."
"What's it feel like to kill a man, Mr. McCoy?" a young woman asks the man across the desk from her...
"Why are you here?" asks the man as the cough fit resides. His eyes are black and moist.
"Why are you worried about the lieutenant-yefreitor?" asks the boy. Then a wave comes crashing in.
"Yes, he lost his mind," Tillbrook answers, finger on the trigger. "Don't worry, Émile..." He pulls on it slowly. Slowly now... "He'll find it again."

"You seen Apricot -- old Pidieu's daughter?" asks Lieutentant McCoy. "Uh-huh," replies Torson. "The ass on that one..." McCoy shakes his head in appreciation. "A bit strange the old man named her Apricot, but, I mean -- who am I to judge? Wanna hit the kebab joint?"
